title: Logical Access Controls — Authentication
description: The organisation implements multi-factor authentication for all privileged accounts and remote access sessions. Access credentials are provisioned through a centralised identity management system and reviewed on a quarterly basis. Dormant accounts inactive for more than 90 days are automatically disabled pending re-authorisation by the account owner's manager.
evidence: MFA enforcement logs from Okta for the period 01 January 2025 to 31 March 2025 confirm 100% coverage across 342 privileged accounts. Quarterly access review sign-off sheets are retained in SharePoint under folder /Audit/Access-Reviews/Q1-2025.

title: Monitoring of Security Events and Anomalies
description: The organisation is required to monitor system components for anomalous behaviour, security events, and indicators of compromise on a continuous basis. Alerts must be triaged within four hours and escalated to the Security Operations Centre if not resolved within eight hours. Audit logs must be retained for a minimum of twelve months.
evidence: Review of the SIEM configuration identified a gap: log ingestion from three production database servers (db-prod-04, db-prod-05, db-prod-06) was disabled following a migration in January 2025 and had not been re-enabled at the time of audit. Security event coverage was therefore incomplete for the period 18 January 2025 to 14 March 2025.
remediation_notes: Immediate remediation required. Log ingestion must be re-enabled across all production database servers no later than 30 April 2025. A root cause analysis must be submitted to the Audit Committee within 10 business days. Interim compensating controls (manual log review twice daily) are in place until remediation is complete.

title: Use of Cryptography — Data at Rest and in Transit
description: All sensitive data classified as Confidential or Restricted must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher. Cryptographic keys must be managed through a dedicated key management service with automated rotation every 365 days. Use of deprecated protocols including SSL, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1 is strictly prohibited.
evidence: Cryptographic configuration scans conducted by the security engineering team on 15 February 2025 confirmed AES-256 encryption across all production datastores. TLS configuration reports from the load balancer show no use of deprecated protocols. Key Management Service rotation logs confirm automated rotation completed on schedule for all 47 active key aliases.

title: Pseudonymisation and Data Minimisation in Analytics Pipeline
description: Article 32 of the GDPR requires the organisation to implement appropriate technical measures including pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data. The analytics data pipeline must ensure that personal identifiers are replaced with pseudonymous tokens before data is written to the analytics data warehouse. Re-identification must be technically infeasible without access to a separately maintained mapping table accessible only to authorised personnel.
evidence: The primary analytics pipeline for customer behavioural data correctly applies SHA-256 based tokenisation prior to loading into the Snowflake data warehouse. However, an audit of the secondary marketing attribution pipeline revealed that email addresses and IP addresses are retained in plaintext in two intermediate staging tables (stg_campaign_clicks and stg_form_submissions) for up to 72 hours before pseudonymisation is applied.
remediation_notes: Engineering team to apply pseudonymisation at the point of ingestion rather than at the staging-to-warehouse transition. Target remediation date is 31 May 2025. A DPIA addendum has been filed with the DPO office to document the interim risk. The Data Protection Authority has not been notified as the risk is assessed as low probability of harm given the 72-hour retention window and access controls on the staging environment.

title: Audit Log Protection and Integrity
description: PCI DSS Requirement 10.3 mandates that audit logs are protected from destruction and unauthorised modifications. Logs capturing access to cardholder data must be retained for at least 12 months, with the most recent 3 months immediately available for analysis. The log management system must implement file integrity monitoring to detect tampering and generate alerts for any modification or deletion of log data.
evidence: Trustwave QSA confirmed compliance during the annual on-site assessment completed 28 January 2025. AWS CloudTrail and S3 object lock configurations verified. CloudWatch log group retention policies set to 395 days (13 months). Immutable log storage confirmed via S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode. File integrity monitoring alerts tested successfully with simulated log deletion — alert generated within 90 seconds.

title: Workforce Access to Electronic Protected Health Information
description: HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(a)(1) requires covered entities and business associates to implement technical policies and procedures that allow only authorised persons or software programs to access electronic protected health information. Role-based access must be enforced such that each workforce member has access only to the minimum necessary ePHI required to perform their job functions.
evidence: Audit of the clinical records system performed on 20 March 2025 identified 14 active user accounts assigned to the Administrator role that had not been used within the preceding 180 days. Additionally, 3 contractor accounts belonging to individuals whose contracts terminated in December 2024 remained active with full read access to the ePHI database. Combined, these 17 accounts represent a material violation of the minimum necessary access principle.
remediation_notes: All 17 non-compliant accounts disabled on 25 March 2025 by the IT Security team. A formal workforce access review process with 90-day cycle has been implemented using ServiceNow automated workflow. The Privacy Officer has documented this incident in the breach risk assessment register. External legal counsel has advised that HHS notification is not required as there is no evidence of access or disclosure by the identified accounts.

title: Third-Party Cloud Service Security Assessment
description: The organisation must maintain an up-to-date register of all third-party cloud services processing Confidential or Restricted data. Each supplier must undergo an annual security assessment covering their SOC 2 Type II report, penetration testing results, data processing agreements, and incident response procedures. Suppliers with a critical risk rating must be subject to on-site audits at least once every two years.
evidence: The vendor risk register was reviewed and confirmed complete as of 14 February 2025, covering 23 cloud service providers. Valid SOC 2 Type II reports were obtained for all 23 vendors. Data Processing Agreements are in place and current for all vendors. Two vendors (Vendor ID: V-014 and V-019) are classified as critical and were subject to on-site audits in 2024, with the next scheduled audits booked for Q3 2025.
